title: Sharing Central VPC Subnets
description:
## One Network, Many Accounts

A platform team maintains a **central networking account** that owns a VPC with carefully managed subnets (and a Transit Gateway, and Route 53 Resolver rules). They want **multiple application accounts in the same AWS Organization** to launch their workloads **directly into these centrally-managed subnets**, so the network team keeps sole control of routing and IP space while app teams just consume capacity — without duplicating the VPC into each account.

Which service enables this?

options.text: **AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM)**: share the subnets (and TGW, Resolver rules) with the other accounts/OUs so they can launch resources into the shared subnets while ownership stays central.
Recreate an identical copy of the VPC, subnets, and route tables in **each** application account and keep them in sync manually.
Establish **VPC peering** from the central VPC to every application account's own VPC.
Use **IAM cross-account roles** so app accounts can assume into the networking account and run their instances there.

explanation: **AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM)** is built to **share AWS resources across accounts** in an Organization without duplicating them. Sharing **VPC subnets** via RAM enables **shared VPC**: the networking account remains the **owner** of the subnets, route tables, and IP space, while participant accounts can **launch their own resources directly into those subnets**. RAM also shares Transit Gateways, Route 53 Resolver rules, License Manager configs, and more. Option B (copying the VPC into every account) is the duplication the requirement explicitly rejects and creates an ongoing sync/IP-management nightmare. Option C (**VPC peering**) connects *separate* VPCs at the network level — app teams would still own their own VPCs, defeating the goal of one centrally-managed network and adding non-transitive peering sprawl. Option D (cross-account roles) would put app workloads *inside the networking account*, breaking account isolation and billing boundaries — not the intent.

hints.title: Share, don't copy
hints.body: When the goal is many accounts consuming one centrally-owned resource without duplication, think Resource Access Manager.

hints.title: Shared VPC keeps ownership central
hints.body: RAM-shared subnets let app accounts launch into them while the network account still owns routing and IP space.
